{"question_type":"comparison","title":"Go-Back-N vs Selective Repeat ARQ","summary":"Both are sliding-window ARQ schemes, but Go-Back-N retransmits a whole run of unacknowledged packets after a loss, while Selective Repeat retransmits only the specific missing packets.","main_explanation":"The key difference is **what happens when one packet is lost but later packets have already been sent**.

- In **Go-Back-N (GBN)**, the receiver mainly accepts only the next expected packet in order. If packet 3 is lost and packets 4, 5, 6 arrive, those later packets are not useful immediately. After timeout, the sender goes back and retransmits packet 3 **and all later unacknowledged packets**.
- In **Selective Repeat (SR)**, the receiver can keep correctly received out-of-order packets. If packet 3 is lost but 4, 5, 6 arrive correctly, the receiver buffers them and individually acknowledges them. The sender only needs to retransmit **packet 3**.

So the tradeoff is:

- **GBN**: simpler, less receiver complexity, but wastes bandwidth when losses happen.
- **SR**: more efficient under loss, but needs more buffering and more complicated bookkeeping.

A concrete example:

1. Sender sends packets 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.
2. Packet 3 is lost.
3. Packets 4 and 5 still arrive.

With **GBN**:
- Receiver discards 4 and 5 because 3 is missing.
- Receiver keeps acknowledging the last in-order packet.
- Sender eventually times out for packet 3.
- Sender retransmits 3, 4, 5.

With **SR**:
- Receiver buffers 4 and 5.
- Receiver sends ACKs for 4 and 5 individually.
- Window may not fully slide yet because packet 3 is still missing.
- Sender retransmits only packet 3.
- Once 3 arrives, buffered 4 and 5 can now be delivered in order.

A common mistake is to think SR means the application receives packets out of order. Actually, SR may **buffer out-of-order packets**, but reliable delivery to the upper layer is still typically made in order.

Another common mistake is to say GBN always resends everything in the window. More precisely, after timeout it resends **the oldest unacknowledged packet and all later unacknowledged packets**.

Why SR is usually more efficient:

- If losses are rare, both work reasonably well.
- If the link has noticeable loss or long delay, GBN can waste many retransmissions.
- SR avoids resending packets that were already received correctly.

Why GBN is simpler:

- Receiver logic is simpler because it mostly tracks only the next expected sequence number.
- Sender can often use a simpler timer strategy.
- Fewer buffering requirements at the receiver.

In short:

- Choose **GBN** when you want conceptual simplicity.
- Choose **SR** when you want better efficiency during loss.","table":{"headers":["Aspect","Go-Back-N","Selective Repeat"],"rows":[["Receiver behavior","Accepts mainly in-order packets; out-of-order packets are discarded","Accepts and buffers out-of-order packets"],["ACK style","Typically cumulative ACKs","Typically individual ACKs for correctly received packets"],["Retransmission after loss","Retransmit the missing packet and all later unacknowledged packets","Retransmit only the specific missing packet(s)"],["Efficiency and complexity","Lower complexity, but less efficient under loss","Higher complexity, but more efficient under loss"]]},"diagram":{"type":"mermaid","code":"sequenceDiagram
 participant S as Sender
 participant R as Receiver

 rect rgb(245,245,245)
 Note over S,R: Go-Back-N
 S->>R: pkt1
 S->>R: pkt2
 S-xR: pkt3 lost
 S->>R: pkt4
 S->>R: pkt5
 R-->>S: ACK1, ACK2, repeated cumulative ACK
 Note over R: pkt4 and pkt5 discarded as out-of-order
 Note over S: timeout for pkt3
 S->>R: retransmit pkt3, pkt4, pkt5
 end

 rect rgb(235,245,255)
 Note over S,R: Selective Repeat
 S->>R: pkt1
 S->>R: pkt2
 S-xR: pkt3 lost
 S->>R: pkt4
 S->>R: pkt5
 R-->>S: ACK1
 R-->>S: ACK2
 R-->>S: ACK4
 R-->>S: ACK5
 Note over R: pkt4 and pkt5 buffered
 Note over S: retransmit only pkt3
 S->>R: pkt3
 Note over R: now deliver pkt3, pkt4, pkt5 in order
 end"},"elec3120_context":"In ELEC3120 transport-layer material, both protocols are presented as sliding-window methods for handling loss, with Selective Repeat emphasized as more efficient because it avoids unnecessary retransmissions.","exam_tip":"If asked to compare them, mention all four of these: receiver handling of out-of-order packets, ACK type, retransmission rule, and complexity vs efficiency. That usually earns full comparison marks.","check_understanding":"If packets 7, 8, 9 are sent and packet 8 is lost but 9 arrives, what does the receiver do in GBN, and what does it do in SR?","calculation_steps":null,"response_style":"full_explanation"}

retrieved_materials.content:
• Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat both handle loss, while allowing lots 
of packets in flight.
• Selective repeat is more efficient at recovering from failure.
Handling Loss

retrieved_materials.content:
246     CHAPTER 3  •  TRANSPORT LAYER
Go-Back-N or Selective Repeat?
Let us close our study of TCP’s error-recovery mechanism by considering the fol-
lowing question: Is TCP a GBN or an SR protocol? Recall that TCP acknowledg-
ments are cumulative and correctly received but out-of-order segments are not 
individually ACKed by the receiver. Consequently, as shown in Figure 3.33 (see 
also Figure 3.19), the TCP sender need only maintain the smallest sequence number 
of a transmitted but unacknowledged byte (SendBase) and the sequence number 
of the next byte to be sent (NextSeqNum). In this sense, TCP looks a lot like a 
GBN-style protocol. But there are some striking differences between TCP and Go-
Back-N. Many TCP implementations will buffer correctly received but out-of-order 
segments [Stevens 1994]. Consider also what happens when the sender sends a 
sequence of segments 1, 2, . . . , N, and all of the segments arrive in order without 
error at the receiver. Further suppose that the

correctly received at the receiver. We’ll come back to this issue shortly when we 
examine the receiver side of GBN.
• A timeout event. The protocol’s name, “Go-Back-N,” is derived from the sender’s 
behavior in the presence of lost or overly delayed packets. As in the stop-and-wait 
protocol, a timer will again be used to recover from lost data or acknowledgment 
packets. If a timeout occurs, the sender resends all packets that have been previ-
ously sent but that have not yet been acknowledged. Our sender in Figure 3.20 
uses only a single timer, which can be thought of as a timer for the oldest trans-
mitted but not yet acknowledged packet. If an ACK is received but there are still 
additional transmitted but not yet acknowledged packets, the timer is restarted. If 
there are no outstanding, unacknowledged packets, the timer is stopped.
The receiver’s actions in GBN are also simple. If a packet with sequence number 
n is received correctly and is in order (that is, the data la

retrieved_materials.content:
22 shows the operation of the GBN protocol for the case of a window 
size of four packets. Because of this window size limitation, the sender sends pack-
ets 0 through 3 but then must wait for one or more of these packets to be acknowl-
edged before proceeding. As each successive ACK (for example, ACK0 and ACK1) 
is received, the window slides forward and the sender can transmit one new packet 
(pkt4 and pkt5, respectively). On the receiver side, packet 2 is lost and thus packets 
3, 4, and 5 are found to be out of order and are discarded.
Before closing our discussion of GBN, it is worth noting that an implementa-
tion of this protocol in a protocol stack would likely have a structure similar to that 
of the extended FSM in Figure 3.20. The implementation would also likely be in 
the form of various procedures that implement the actions to be taken in response to 
the various events that can occur. In such event-based programming, the various 
procedures are called (invoked) either by
